items.myth: Overfitting means the model is always too complex.
items.reality: Overfitting is poor generalization from fitting training-specific noise — often from excess capacity on limited data, but also from training too long, leaky features, or small-sample variance even with moderate models.
items.example: A medium-sized net trained 50 epochs past the validation minimum overfits without being the largest architecture in the search space.

items.myth: Underfitting and overfitting are mutually exclusive — a model is always one or the other.
items.reality: Diagnosis is regime-based on train vs val signals; a model can be underfit on some structure and overfit noise on other features. Learning curves and per-slice metrics help.
items.example: Large model underfits rare classes (high error everywhere for them) while overfitting frequent head classes (low train, higher val gap).

items.reality: Zero training error is a warning sign on noisy real-world data, but separable noiseless tasks can legitimately reach zero train and val error with adequate capacity.
items.example: MNIST with a well-sized CNN can approach 0% train and ~0.5% test — not overfitting.

items.reality: Overfitting depends on parameters relative to data, noise, and regularization — large models with enough data, early stopping, or weight decay can generalize well.
items.example: Modern LLMs are huge yet generalize when trained on massive diverse corpora with regularization and careful evaluation.
